2024 · CVPR 2024 · Benchmarks & Evals

MMMU: A Massive Multi-Discipline Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark for Expert AGI

11,550 college-exam questions across 6 disciplines, 30 subjects and 30 image types — diagrams, chemical structures, music scores, MRIs, circuit schematics — that demand expert knowledge plus deliberate reasoning. At launch the best open models managed ~34% and GPT-4V 55.7%; human experts, 88.6%.

Xiang Yue, Yuansheng Ni, Kai Zhang, Tianyu Zheng, … Huan Sun, Yu Su, Wenhu Chen
venue CVPR 2024 citations 2,388 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2023, multimodal models looked nearly solved on the benchmarks that existed. CogVLM scored 85% on VQA-v2, 92% on ScienceQA-IMG, and 93% on RefCOCO. But those suites test everyday perception and commonsense — "what's in this photo," "is the sky blue" — not the kind of specialized seeing-and-reasoning a trained professional does. The depth lived in text-only exam benchmarks like MMLU and AGIEval, which probe college-level knowledge but show the model no images at all.

So nothing measured the obvious next question: can a vision-language model do what a skilled adult specialist does — read a circuit schematic, a Punnett square, a breast MRI, a four-bar music phrase — and reason to a college-level answer? The closest prior multimodal-and-multidiscipline option, ScienceQA, spanned subjects but sat mostly at the elementary-to-middle-school level. MMMU frames the target around Morris et al.'s leveled taxonomy of AGI, where "Expert AGI" (Level 3) is an AI at the 90th percentile of skilled adults. College exams — built precisely to certify skilled adults per discipline — are the natural yardstick, and MMMU is the multimodal version that prior text-only exam suites left out.

// how it works

What's in the benchmark

MMMU is 11,550 questions spanning six disciplines — Art & Design, Business, Science, Health & Medicine, Humanities & Social Science, and Tech & Engineering — broken into 30 subjects and 183 subfields. It deliberately stresses two things prior suites skipped. First, 30 heterogeneous image types: not just photographs but diagrams, tables, charts, chemical structures, paintings, medical scans, sheet music, geometric figures, pathology slides, microscopy, comics. Second, interleaved text and images — a question can carry several images (<image 1>, <image 2>…) referenced inline, so a model must fuse the two modalities rather than caption a picture. Together these target three abilities: perception, knowledge, and reasoning.

How it was built

A team of 50 college students (including coauthors), each specializing in one of the 30 subjects, collected questions from textbooks, exams, quizzes and lecture materials, authoring new ones where needed. That produced ~13K raw questions, cleaned down to 11.5K through three passes: deduplication by lexical overlap and source-URL similarity, format/typo checking, and a four-level difficulty labeling that dropped ~10% of items as "very easy." To blunt contamination, annotators were told to favor questions whose answers are not immediately adjacent — answers kept in a separate document or at the back of the textbook — so the model can't have trivially memorized the answer alongside the question.

Splits, format and scoring

The set divides into a dev split of 150 (5 per subject, for few-shot prompts), a validation split of 900 (for hyperparameter/prompt selection), and a test split of 10,500. About 94% are multiple-choice (10,861) and 6% open-ended (689); 97.5% include at least one image, and 7.4% carry multiple images. The difficulty mix is 28% easy / 45% medium / 27% hard. Evaluation is zero-shot, scored by micro-averaged accuracy, with rule-based regular expressions that pull the final answer out of long, free-form responses. If no valid answer can be parsed, MMMU performs a random selection for multiple-choice questions (and marks open questions wrong) — a detail that matters for reading the numbers below. Random-Choice and Frequent-Choice baselines anchor the floor.

// what they found

The results

At submission (late 2023) the benchmark was brutal. The best open-source models — BLIP-2 FLAN-T5-XXL and LLaVA-1.5 — landed around 34%. The strongest proprietary model, GPT-4V, reached 55.7% on the test set (56.8% on validation), with Gemini Ultra reported at ~59%. Against that, human experts scored 88.6% (best of three per subject, open-book on the 900 validation questions) — a wide, unmistakable gap. Random choice sits at 23.9%.

The most revealing row isn't a vision model at all. GPT-4 with no image input scored 33.8% on test (34.9% val) — statistically tied with the best image-seeing open-source models. And bolting OCR or LLaVA-generated captions onto text-only LLMs barely helped (FLAN-T5-XXL 31.2% → ~31.9%). That cuts two ways: 2023-era open LMMs were barely exploiting their visual channel, and a meaningful share of MMMU is answerable from the question text, answer options and parametric knowledge alone.

ModelValTest

Frequent Choice26.825.8
GPT-4 — text only, no image34.933.8
BLIP-2 FLAN-T5-XXL (best open @ launch)35.434.0
LLaVA-1.6-34B *51.144.7
InternVL-Chat-V1.2 *51.646.2
GPT-4V(ision)56.855.7
Claude 3 Opus *59.4—
Gemini 1.5 Pro *62.2—
GPT-4o *69.1—
Human Expert (best) — open-book88.6—

// Selected rows from Table 2 (accuracy %). * = scores self-reported by the model's authors, not run by the MMMU team under one harness; “—” = test score not reported.

Two more cuts sharpen the picture. By difficulty, GPT-4V drops from 76.1% (Easy) to 55.6% (Medium) to 31.2% (Hard) — and on the Hard slice GPT-4V (31.2%) sits barely above open models (~26–29%) and the 24% random floor — its big Easy-slice lead has all but vanished. By image type, every model is near-random on Geometric shapes, Music sheets and Chemical structures, while doing better on Photos and Paintings that resemble pretraining data. By discipline, GPT-4V is strongest in Humanities & Social Science (76.3%) and weakest in Tech & Engineering (41.7%) and Science (48.4%), where visuals are dense and reasoning is heavy.

A hand-coded analysis of 150 GPT-4V errors traces the failures to 35% perceptual, 29% lack of knowledge, 26% flawed reasoning (the rest: textual understanding 4%, refusal 3%, annotation error 2%, answer-extraction 1%). The model also shows a text bias — leaning on textual cues over what the image actually shows. Over the following year, open models closed much of the gap (LLaVA-1.6-34B 44.7%, InternVL-Chat-V1.2 46.2% test) and frontier models climbed (Claude 3 Opus 59.4, Gemini 1.5 Pro 62.2, GPT-4o 69.1 on validation), with MMMU becoming the de-facto multimodal leaderboard.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The sharpest issue is construct validity for "multimodal." GPT-4 with no image (33.8% test) matches the best vision-equipped open models, and OCR/caption augmentation of blind LLMs barely helps. To MMMU's credit, it reports that blind baseline — but it doesn't act on it. A non-trivial share of items is solvable from question text, answer options and parametric knowledge, so part of the "multimodal" score is really a text-knowledge exam. The honest fix is to measure per-item blind-LLM solvability and release a vision-necessary subset; without it, a single number conflates "can see and reason" with "can guess from the prompt."

There's also a scoring confound baked into the metric. Unparseable responses get a random answer on multiple-choice (and are marked wrong on open questions). With 94% multiple-choice and regex extraction over long chain-of-thought outputs, reported accuracy blends genuine correctness with extraction luck and the ~24% random floor — so format-following, not just reasoning, leaks into the score, and differently-styled model families aren't strictly comparable. And the headline micro-average flatters the frontier: it's carried by the Easy slice, while on Hard questions and on Music/Chemical/Geometric image types even GPT-4V sits near chance.

baselines

Broad and thoughtful — 28 open models plus GPT-4V/Gemini, text-only LLM and OCR/caption probes, and Random/Frequent floors. But many frontier rows (Yi-VL, InternVL, Qwen-VL-MAX, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4o) are author-reported, validation-only, and not run under one harness, so cross-model gaps are soft.

contamination

Questions come from textbooks and the open web — a real pretraining-ingestion vector. The mitigation — prefer questions whose answers aren't immediately adjacent — is sensible but weak against ingestion, and while test answers sit behind a leaderboard, there's no private, periodically-refreshed split, so scores can drift upward through contamination over time.

what's missed

A micro-average hides that the Hard slice is near-random for everyone (GPT-4V 31.2 vs 24 random) and that some image types are near-chance — and that GPT-4 blind (33.8) ties the best vision models, i.e. part of the test is text-only.

would raise confidence

Report per-item blind-LLM solvability and ship a vision-necessary subset; replace random-fill with an explicit abstention/parse-fail category; publish per-discipline and per-image-type confidence intervals; maintain a private, periodically-refreshed test split; and use closed-book domain experts (not open-book seniors) for the human ceiling.

// bottom line

MMMU did what a field-defining benchmark should: it reset the multimodal ceiling from "solved" (90%+ on VQA-style sets) to "wide open" (GPT-4V 55.7, humans 88.6) and gave the community a broad, expert-level target that became the default multimodal leaderboard. But the headline accuracy oversells — a large blind-LLM baseline, a random-fill scoring floor, near-chance Hard performance, and a public, contaminable test set make the single number a loose proxy. Read MMMU as a coarse altitude gauge for frontier multimodal models, not a precise or contamination-proof measure of expert reasoning.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. MMMU is the multimodal version of MMLU. 11.5K college-level questions across 6 disciplines / 30 subjects / 30 image types, scored zero-shot by micro-averaged accuracy — built to track progress toward "Expert AGI" (90th-percentile skilled adult), not everyday perception.
  2. At launch it reset the bar and exposed the field. Best open models ~34%, GPT-4V 55.7%, vs human experts 88.6% — and crucially GPT-4 with no image hit 33.8%, matching vision models and revealing how text-solvable (and how vision-underused) early multimodal models really were.
  3. The headline number is a loose proxy. Random-fill scoring, near-random Hard-slice performance, author-reported frontier scores, and a public/contaminable test set all inflate or blur it. But as the de-facto multimodal leaderboard (GPT-4o later 69.1), MMMU's breadth and difficulty made it the standard climb.
